This example question was taken from a lesson teaching unit-test writing in Ruby. In it, the students are asked to write a test suite for the `GiftCard` class, and the autograder introduces bugs for them to catch. This example demonstrates how pl-faded-parsons can be used with auto-graders other than Python, and can also serve as an example for auto-grading unit tests written by students. *Note: we disabled the line randomization and place-held the answers for demo purposes.*
Here is part of a class that models a gift card: ```ruby class GiftCard attr_reader :balance, :error def initialize(balance) if balance < 0 raise ArgumentError.new("New gift card cannot have negative balance") end @balance = balance @error = "" end # ... end ``` We perform assertions with RSpec by wrapping objects with `expect` and checking if a condition supplied by calling `to` on the wrapped object is met. For example, we can check if `@my_object` can `meet_my_condition` by writing: `expect(@my_object).to meet_my_condition`. In this question, construct tests that verify: * a GiftCard is created successfully with a nonnegative balance * a GiftCard cannot be created with a negative balance --- describe GiftCard do it 'fails to instantiate with negative balance' do #pin __(expect)__ { GiftCard.new(-1) }.to raise_error(ArgumentError) #pin(1) end #pin it 'successfully assigns a positive balance on instantiation' do #pin gift_card = __(GiftCard)__.new(20) #pin(1) expect(gift_card.balance).to eq(20) #pin(1) end #pin end

Note: It may take up to 30 seconds for the auto-grader to display results after you press "Save & Grade".
